name: Sicilian Defense
description: The most popular response to 1.e4 at the top level. Black creates an asymmetric position and fights for counterplay from the start.

moves.commentary: King's pawn opening.
The Sicilian Defense — Black fights for the center asymmetrically. Creates imbalance from move one.
Develops the knight and prepares to open the center.
Supports e5 and keeps options open for the knight development.
Opens the center. White gives up the d-pawn to gain a lead in development.
Black captures, accepting the open c-file for future counterplay.
Recaptures with the knight, centralized and powerful.
Attacks e4 while developing.
Defends e4 and develops the last minor piece.
The Najdorf! Prepares b5 expansion and prevents Bb5 pins. Fischer's weapon of choice.
A solid, classical approach. Develops the bishop before committing to an aggressive plan.
Black stakes out space in the center and kicks the knight.
The knight retreats. From b3 it supports d4 and controls c5.
Develops and prepares to castle. Black has a flexible, fighting position.
King's pawn.
Sicilian Defense.
Develops and prepares d4.
Supports a future e5 or prepares Nf6.
Opens the center.
Captures.
Centralizes the knight.
Attacks e4.
Defends e4.
The Dragon! Black fianchettoes the bishop to g7, creating a 'dragon' formation. One of the sharpest openings in chess.
Develops and prepares Qd2 for the Yugoslav Attack.
The dragon bishop — aims along the long diagonal toward White's queenside.
Supports e4 and prepares Qd2 + O-O-O. The Yugoslav Attack is the most critical test of the Dragon.
Black castles into the storm. Both sides will launch opposite-side attacks.
Connects the rooks and prepares O-O-O + Bh6 to trade off the dragon bishop.
Develops and pressures d4. A razor-sharp battle ahead with mutual attacks.

The Scheveningen — Black sets up a small pawn center with d6+e6. Flexible but slightly passive. Kasparov's favorite structure.
Classical development. White keeps options open between kingside and queenside strategies.
Black develops smoothly, preparing to castle.
White castles and prepares the middlegame plan.
Both sides castled kingside. A rich strategic battle ahead.
White gains space on the kingside. Typical Scheveningen plan — pressure along the f-file.
Develops the last minor piece and pressures the central knight.

The Classical Sicilian — develops the knight immediately rather than choosing a6, g6, or e6. Direct and logical.
Solid development. White prepares to castle before committing to an attack plan.
Black strikes the center, gaining space and challenging the d4 knight.
Retreats to b3, maintaining control of d4 from a distance.
Develops the bishop and prepares to castle. A typical Classical setup.
White castles. Plans include f4 or Be3 + Nd5.
Black castles. A balanced middlegame with chances for both sides.
